t101:
Existential risks are threats to the very survival of our species. Closely related are global catastrophic risks - risks that threaten to significantly reduce the amount of population over a short time span. 

For both of these types, the threats that are often brought up include: 
- Artificial intelligence
- Engineeded pandemics
- Nuclear weapons
- Biological weapons
- Climate change
- Asteroid impact and other natural phenomena

Studies on the topic usually focus on probability estimates given by experts on either the individual risks or the total risk levels. Perhaps the most comprehensive queastionnaire study on topic by the Forecasting Research Institure presents median total extinction probabilities from 1% to 7% by year 2100, depending on the expert group*. Catastrophic risk probabilities, estimated by the same experts, ranged from 9% to 27%. 


Another focus on this topic is in the grand implications of the risk in general, especially for existential risk. The future can be vast and the welfare it holds immense, and we have the responsibility to uphold its possibility. Even small probabilities have great implications for the expected value of the future. 

*XPT tournament.... Extinction was defined as reduction of population to 5000 persons or less. Catastrophic risk was defined as... Page. 
Extinction was defined as
t102:
In times of limited resources and increasing levels of power, conflicts can become both more likely and more destructive. 

It is now more important than ever to settle disagreements peacefully and to work together as a global whole. 

There are good prerequisites for improving our outlook. Techonological progress produces more threats but also more sustainable ways to reach a decent standard of living.
t103:
European Union is an example of close and continuous coordination between countries - negotiaation and decisionmaking. It is important for it to succeed to motivate that kind of co-operation in a larger scale. 

European countries themselves are stronger together at the world's stage. EU should use it's power to create beneficial global structures. 

(Linked to Global harmony)
t104:
My interest in climate change is motivated especially by the danger it poses to civilizational stability. Climate change can increase the probability and severity of conflicts. 

(Linked to Existential and catastrophic risks and Global harmony)
t105:
Electrification of energy use is important for stopping climate change, because electricity can be produced with very low emission sources such as nuclear, hydro, wind and solar. 

Wind and solar are cheap to produce, but their variable production causes occassionally very high price spikes. Making clean electicity cheap enough is important for its adoption.

I am interested in this theme especially at the level of Finland and the European Union. 

(Linked to climate change)
t106:
It is very important that Ukraine defends itself against Russia both for Ukraine's independence and survival, but also for its impact on Russia's willingness to be aggressive against its neighbours in the future. Countries' contributions to Ukraine's victory also send a broader message that aggressors are punished and defenders supported. This should create a peace-building dynamic. 

(Linked to Global harmony)
p201:
See link for more info: https://villeseppala.wordpress.com/2025/07/07/website-concept-for-visualizing-existential-risk-looking-for-feedback-funding/


In short, the site allows the users to set input values for existential risk probability for various time periods (or survival probability at given points). It then calculates the expected longevity of humankind (measured in expected years the humankind will exist or in expected human lives. The graphs show the results in both the condensed and linear time scales. 

The motivation behind the site is to demonstrate that even very small improvements in existential risk will pay off immensely in the long run.
p202:
(medium size text, possibly an article or a blog)

Related to previous project.

###Issues with the standard approach

Most questionnaire studies concerning individual existential risks (such as artificial intelligence, engineered pandemics, nuclear weapons, climate change etc.) ask the respondents to estimate the probability of them causing humankind's extinction or a civilizational collapse. There are major issues with this approach, sometimes noted in the literature. Risks are intertwined and it is also difficult to determine what is the threshold of contribution from a specific threat for it to be considered as a cause for an extinction/catastrophic event in the questionnaire setting. To illustrate: If an extinction event is caused by a misaligned AI using nuclear weapons, is it an AI or nuclear extinction? Most of the questionnaires allow it to be both. But if AI is used only very partially, in a very mundane capacity, does it still count? Climate change poses an even bigger complexity. It is very unlikely that climate change in itself would cause extinction, but it may increase the probability of major conflicts and therefore, it could be a major factor behind extinction even if it wasn’t the final mechanism for it. But often in the literature it seems that this is not taken into account in the threat estimates from climate change. All in all, I consider that there is considerable ambiguity in these settings.

###Risk factors and an approach based on overall risk reduction

Toby Ord explores these issues to some extent in The Precipice (2020) and talks about risk factors that influence the risks themselves. I would like to explore this further and consider if it would be wise to evaluate the threats by asking how much the removal of a threat (risk factor) altogether would reduce the overall existential/catastrophic risk probability. This estimate would indicate the overall impact of a threat (regardless of how deeply entrenched to other threats the threat is), while taking into account the impact from other threats (thus avoiding double counting issues when comparing risks). Focus on risk factors and their reduction would also be a more policy relevant approach.

###Practical considerations

I consider that there are two ways to estimate the risk reduction probability. Firstly, in percentage points, as absolute reduction. In this case, if a threat was totally uncorrelated to other threats (such as m maybe asteroid impacts), the reduction estimate would also correspond to the standard risk probability estimate from the risk.

Second way is to estimate the reduction relative to the overall risk probability, a percentage reduction than a percentage point reduction. This could be preferable especially if the overall risk is hard to estimate but the relative magnitude of the specific risk relative to others, and the risk interaction to relative to them, is clearer. Questionnaires could be built to accomodate both ways.
p203:
(medium size text, possibly article)

I feel that when punitive tariffs are used, they are usually based on general disagreements between countries. They might lead to a chain of retaliatory tariffs and worsen relations between the countries even more.

Encouraging countries to set tariffs based on specific issues instead of antagonism could be more productive. The tariff setter would have to specify a measurable topic the tariff is based on and could not hide behind ambiguity. For fairness, the setter should apply the same metric to all the other countries as well, and set the tariffs on all that meet the criteria. Explicit metric would also let other countries to better evaluate how fair the tariffs are and whether to join the tariff setter in their implementation, as part of a tariff union. The tariff receivers would know what to change in order to get the tariffs taken off or reduced. This would make the tariffs to be guiding.

Encouraging this kind of tariffs could help to move the world from blocs and into overlapping tariff unions on various topics. Setting tariffs would become less dramatic, more business as usual. This could further relieve retaliatory pressure. This, in turn, would lower the threshold to set tariffs and bring the tariff topics more into international and national political discussion and make them a widely used global political tool.

A prototype example of this kind of tariff is the EU's carbon border adjustment mechanism (CBAM) starting gradually in 2026. It sets a tariff type payment on imports from some product classes if their carbon contents have not been priced equally to EU's emission trading system by the source country. The payment size is relative to the difference between the carbon price paid in source country and the EU-ETS carbon price.

Other examples that could be basis for tariffs:

- Corporate income tax

- Participation and compliance on various nuclear treaties

- Refugee intake rates

- Press freedom indices

Not sure yet if it would be optimal to let countries set within unions to set tariff rates separately, or would it be better if the rates were equal.

p205: EU Climate architecture following year 2030 is currently being planned and there are several options of approach. Regardless, there will probably be some kind of sectoral divide and, for some sector/sectors, national emission targets. I would like to build a website that allows the user to set an EU wide goal and then divide it to sectoral targets and national emission targets. User can choose the targets very manually or through some set of weightable criteria, relating to current emissions levels and gdp, for example. The tool could be used to assist negotiations and to refrelt.
p205.5: If humanity were to live in a sustainable fashion, what kind of average resource use and lifestyle will it allow us?
p206:
Carbon pricing is an efficient way to reduce emissions, but causes a direct cost to consumers, which often increases resistance to it. Also, the benefits of the carbon pricing (like taxation in general) in terms of government revenue are often hidden to the public. I’d like to make a video that explains the efficiency of carbon pricing and its benefits as a way to create government revenue and welfare. Efficiency compared to other means of emission reductions is due to market mechanism automatically incentivizing emission reductions in where they are cheapest to perform, with some caveats with regards to innovation externalilities and to markets not reaching some areas that governments have a executive power over.

A portion of the video would explore the possibility of global carbon pricing and dividend, using my website www.globalcarbonprice.com to showcase what kind of effects the system would have with different emission progression and carbon price specifications.

p207:
(Shorter text, a blog)

###Problem and motivation

Sweden and Norway (hereafter, the North) have shown resistance to building new power interconnectors to Central Europe. (links here). They would increase the average electricity prices in these countries, since there is more demand for electricity in the Central Europe. However, they would benefit Europe as a whole. The population weighted decrease in electricity prices in Central Europe would be greater than the increase in the North.

Decreasing electricity prices across Europe is important for Europe's industrial competetiveness.

Could EU or the European countries within the same power market create a system to encourage building new interconnectors?

###My suggested solution (in progress)

My idea for this: Countries who import electricity at any moment would pay to the countries that export it to them? The size of payment would be determined by the contrafactual electricity prices for the case that there was no interconnector between the countries. This indicates the benefit of the interconnector to the importer and the harm to the exporter. The payment would not be 100% of the difference, since the exporting country benefits by having a greater power generation industry to tax. And because their own prices increase less than is the decrease in importing country.

This soluction would also incentivize countries to remove bureaucratical barriers from building new electricity generation if it is cheaper than payments to importers. Would decrease average electricity prices.

This would incentivize building flexible power generation wherever possible, since it would fetch a better price than currently. Which would decrease average prices.

The momentary wind energy production varies a lot more across Europe than across a single country. Stronger power connections between countries would help to balance this variation. This would make wind energy capture rates be higher. It is important that we can take the max. benefit from wind energy, since it is so cheap to produce.

They would incentivize countries to build steady production electricity, since that would decrease their dependence from exporting countries when electricity is expensive.
p208:
(medium size text, possibly an article)

Would pumped-storage hydro power be a good solution for Finland? Considering some of the following aspects:

* Economic benefits. What would be the effect on energy prices. Would the projects need government support?
* How much room is there for capacity?
* How much harm would it cause to nature and how much resources need? Compared to regular hydro and battey storage solutions.

Currently, I think so.
p209:
I have made several Nightingale/radial plots for comparing countries across different topics/outcomes. Some can be seen here: https://villeseppala.wordpress.com/pictures-and-animations/
[Link text](https://example.com)  

I’d like to make producing them more automatic and produce a website for it, so that the user can select what outcome variable to look at, how to group countries (color the bars), and what year to compare to track change over time.
p209.5:
I have uploaded some of the data visualizations I've created here:

Some of them I've updated at some points in time. Updating can be quite straigt forward if data is machine readable from web and in same form as before. However, uploading and hosting the graphs takes some time. Also, I have to do both updating and hosting separately for each graph. 

I would like to create a script that automates most of this work. So that running it would fetch the latest data, create updated graphs and host them on the site. In R, ggplot2 and quarto probably integrate quite nicely for this end.  

Many of the graphs in the graph bank are outdated also in their design. Having an automation system would motivate me to redesign some of them, since I would know they would remain current in the future.
p210: Creating a homepage. Currently I'm using a wordpress base for my website at villeseppala.fi. Quarto/Markdown based website would allow more flexibility and better use of ggplot2 graphs and changes thourgh github, for example.

s303:
I watch a lot of youtube, and understand its possibilites in reaching new audiences and providing an efficient way to communicate your ideas. On the other hand, I feel that videos take a lot of time to produce, which fits a bit badly with my tendency to not always follow through with my projects.  

I haven't done videos for a while, and the ones I have done have focused on showing graphs in some way. This would probably be the case in future as well, but in addition I would include other graphical elements to visualize stuff. 

Streaming, on the other hand, is quite effortless. I have sometimes streamed while working on some project. It is difficult to make this kind of thing entertaining and gain audience though, but if I managed, it could pay off through getting constant feedback.
s304:
I have started exploring the capabilities of AI tools and want to continue it, based on my good impressions on their capabilities and the general hype around them.
I have mixed feelings about AI in general. I share the usual worries people have regarding AI's threats to society. And for my own productivity, about how it lessens my understanding of how my code works, for example. 

I also feel that the genie is out of the bottle, and not using AI can put you at a disadvantage on various avenus. Using AI can also better help you understand what kind of dangers it poses.
s305: I want to partake more in social media to gain audience. Also about figuring out what channels are best and most impactful.
